home.leadHeadlineTail: who'd rather solve the problem than decorate the screen.
home.clientNote: Real clients, real work. The pixels just aren't mine to share.

explorations.lead: PERSONAL TOOLS, SIDE QUESTS, AND HALF-FORMED IDEAS
explorations.leadEm: made to answer my own questions.

cta.heading: So — what are we solving?
cta.sub: Freelance, a partnership, or a build that needs a designer who ships. Tell me what you're working on.

hero.title: Rebuilding the AmaliTech website from the ground up
hero.description: A WordPress template had outgrown the company. I led the move to a CMS-backed site, building the design system, tone, and editorial guidelines from scratch so the team could publish without a designer in the loop.

clients.description: I'm redesigning several of their platforms onto the OneDesign system, tightening the UX as I go. An ongoing partner engagement I'm quietly proud of.
I modernised their platform's UI in a short, focused engagement. Small team, tight scope, real dent.

products.title: A video conferencing platform
products.description: Designed the calling experience end to end: joining, presence, and the small moments between calls that most tools ignore.

products.title: Meeting room booking
products.description: A booking system that makes finding and holding a room take seconds, not clicks.

explorationProjects.title: Your phone as a trackpad
explorationProjects.description: Controlling a laptop from across the room shouldn't need a manual. I designed the gestures to feel obvious on first touch.

explorationProjects.title: Media that learns your taste
explorationProjects.description: Most feeds just replay what you've already saved. Weaver reads your taste from what you've engaged with, then goes looking for new work you haven't seen, ranked by how close it lands.

explorations.title: Learning a car, part by part
explorations.description: New drivers pick a car, open the bonnet, and read up on each part: what it does, upkeep, cost.

about.metaDescription: I work with teams at whatever stage they're in. What stays constant is where I start: the problem, before the pixels.
about.lead: I work with teams at whatever stage they're in: an idea with no shape yet, a product due for a rethink, or one that works but needs to work harder. What stays constant is where I start:

sections.heading: What I do
body: Product design is what I do most, across web, mobile, and desktop. It comes with website design, design systems, prototyping, and often the build itself when a project calls for it. No two get the same answer, because no two start from the same place.

sections.heading: Right now
body: I'm a UI/UX designer at AmaliTech, working out of Takoradi, Ghana. I design in Figma and build in the browser when it helps the idea land.

sections.heading: How I work
body: I believe the best design takes time and requires room to sit with the problem before solving it, not a sprint to a deadline.
And I like to work in the open. I keep the people I'm building with close and ask questions early, rather than guess. The clearer we both are, the better the thing we end up making.

sections.heading: PARALLEL INTERESTS
body: Behind a camera, or deep in a 3D scene modelling and sculpting, chasing the same thing I chase at work: how a thing is made, and how it makes you feel.

reading.title: Favorite books
reading.note: Books I keep coming back to. Hover or tap one for the line that stuck.

books.title: East of Eden
books.author: John Steinbeck
books.quote: And now that you don't have to be perfect, you can be good.

amalitech.title: Rebuilding the AmaliTech website from the ground up
amalitech.summary: The WordPress template had outgrown its uses and it was time to create a full-fledged website from scratch. I led the design aspects in the move to a CMS-backed site, taking charge of the design system, tone of voice, and editorial guidelines — so the team could publish on-brand without a designer in the loop.
highlights: Moved off an outgrown WordPress template onto a CMS the team owns.
A design system, tone of voice, and editorial guidelines, all built from scratch.
The team now publishes on-brand without a designer in the loop.

hero.alt: The rebuilt AmaliTech website home page
hero.caption: The rebuilt home page — one system, published by the team itself.

sections.heading: The problem
sections.body: AmaliTech had outgrown its website. A WordPress template picked for speed early on had, over time, started shaping the company more than the company shaped it: new pages bent to what the template allowed, brand details drifted, and anything past a basic edit needed developer intervention. The site had become a bottleneck instead of a platform.

sections.heading: The approach
sections.body: I treated it as a systems problem, not a reskin. Two goals sat under every decision: give the team a site they could actually run themselves, and make staying on-brand the path of least resistance. That meant coming up with CMS modules the team owned, and the design language, voice, and rules to hold the site together long after launch. I conducted UX research, collected and improved the modules and pages based on user feedback.

sections.heading: Team and my role
sections.body: As lead designer, I worked across the project on more than one front:
list: Led the design hands-on — the system, the pages, and the details in between.
Ran the UX research end to end, then systemised scattered feedback into changes the team could act on.
Worked in the open with engineers, content, product, and marketing — designing with them, not handing work over a wall.
Started a design-critique rhythm with the other designers, so consistency held across everyone's work, not just mine.

sections.heading: What I built
list: A design system from scratch: type, colour, spacing, and a component set covering the pages the company actually publishes.
A tone of voice — how AmaliTech sounds in a headline, a caption, a call to action — so the copy reads as one company.
Editorial and content guidelines: what goes where, how to structure a page, and the do's and don'ts that keep new pages honest.
CMS templates and page patterns mapped to the system, so building a page means assembling blocks, not designing one.

media.alt: The services page: a six-card capability grid built from one card component
media.caption: One card component, six services, zero one-off layouts — the grid, icons, links and CTA all come from the system.

sections.heading: Publishing without a designer
sections.body: The real deliverable wasn't simply the launch — it was the team's ability to keep going. With the components, the voice, and the guidelines in place, the team publishes new pages on its own and going off-brand is hard to do by accident. The design work is baked into the tools, so it doesn't leave after the development team is done.

media.alt: The CMS-backed insights index: search, tags, and a card grid of dated articles
media.caption: The team publishes here whenever they want; same article template, filters and category system, no designer needed in the loop.

sections.heading: The outcome
sections.body: The rebuilt site launched on the new system, and time on site rose around 15% after the change — a site people stayed with rather than bounced off. More quietly, the publishing bottleneck went away: the team ships pages at its own pace.

media.alt: An engagement-model accordion flowing into a client and partner logo wall
media.caption: One expandable component carries three engagement models — and the client wall shows the system ships real business.

sections.heading: What I'd do differently
sections.body: A system is only as strong as the discipline around it. Running it again, I'd stand up a light governance rhythm from day one — a clear owner for the component set and a short review cadence — so the system keeps its edges as the site grows, instead of leaving that to catch up later.

trackpad.title: Your phone as a trackpad
trackpad.summary: Your phone as a trackpad, and a remote for the whole laptop. Point, scroll, type, and nudge the volume from across the room, with none of it needing a manual.
highlights: Your phone becomes a full trackpad: move, click, scroll, cycle apps.
Pairing is a QR scan; direct and low-latency on your own Wi-Fi.
Off-network relay that never sees your keys.

hero.alt: The LazeR Android control screen and its settings sheet, recreated from the app
hero.caption: The control surface: trackpad and scroll strip, media transport, and the settings that tune the feel.

sections.heading: The problem
sections.body: Controlling a laptop from the sofa usually means a flaky app, a fiddly setup, or a menu you have to learn. Every option asked me to learn a new remote when the best pointer I own was already in my hand.

sections.heading: The approach
sections.body: I wanted the phone to feel like an extension of the trackpad you already know: obvious on first touch, nothing to read. Every gesture maps to something your thumb already does, so the learning curve is the one you climbed years ago.

sections.heading: The gestures
list: Drag to move, tap to click, with optional pointer acceleration so it tracks like a real trackpad.
Two fingers to scroll; swipe them sideways for browser back and forward.
Three fingers to cycle apps, the way a laptop touchpad does.
Sliders for system volume and brightness, synced both ways.
A keyboard panel and one-shot clipboard paste, the typing a phone is actually good at.

media.alt: The full-bleed trackpad expanded, and the keyboard panel for typing on the laptop
media.caption: The pad goes full-bleed when you want room; a keyboard panel handles the typing a phone is actually good at.

sections.heading: Getting out of the way
sections.body: Pairing is a QR scan, not a config screen. On the same Wi-Fi it stays direct and low-latency; off your network it still connects through a coordinator that never sees your keys. Haptics confirm every tap, and the phone eases off polling when you are idle to spare the battery.

media.alt: The desktop server's pairing screen — a device card and a QR code to scan
media.caption: The desktop side of pairing: one QR scan, no config screen.

sections.heading: What I'd do differently
sections.body: The off-network rendezvous coordinator is the part I would rethink. Powerful, but heavy for what most people actually want, which is the sofa. I would ship the same-network path first and treat remote access as an opt-in, not a reason to stand up infrastructure.

video-conferencing.title: A video conferencing platform
video-conferencing.summary: A calling app built end to end. Not the grid of faces, but everything around it: how you arrive, how presence reads at a glance, and the small moments most tools drop on the floor.
highlights: The whole call considered: arriving, presence, and leaving, not just the video grid.
Built from a small set of primitives, so every surface behaves the same way.
Accessibility wired into CI, not bolted on at the end.

hero.alt: The pre-call lobby: a camera preview, device toggles, and a mic level meter
hero.caption: The pre-call lobby — a device check and a live mic meter before you commit to joining.

sections.heading: The problem
sections.body: Most video tools nail the call and forget the edges. Joining is anxious, you can't tell who's really present, and the moments between talking (a reaction, stepping away, leaving quietly) feel clumsy. I wanted the whole experience considered, not just the video feed.

media.alt: The landing screen: start a new meeting or join one by name or code
media.caption: The front door — one screen to start or join. The anxious part (am I in the right place? who's here?) is where most tools drop you cold.

sections.heading: The approach
sections.body: I built the interface from a small set of primitives (dialog, sheet, popover, tooltip, a stage) so every surface behaves the same way. The call sits at the center, but the design work lives in the edges: arriving, reading the room, and leaving without a fuss.

media.alt: The passwordless sign-in dialog: continue with Google, or an email sign-in link
media.caption: Sign-in is a dialog like every other surface — same primitive, passwordless, so arriving is one tap, not a form.

sections.heading: The small moments
list: Presence you can read at a glance: who is here, muted, speaking, or away.
Reactions and call sounds that add warmth without hijacking the call.
Picture-in-picture and media-session controls, so a call survives switching tabs.
Background blur and noise suppression for focus, one toggle away.
Moderation built in: blocks and mutes that actually stick.

sections.heading: Craft
sections.body: Accessibility was a foundation, not a final pass: keyboard paths, a live announcer for state changes, and automated a11y checks in CI. Mobile got its own UX pass, and end-to-end and capacity tests keep it honest under a full room.

media.alt: Appearance settings: theme colour tokens, high-contrast, and vision-assistive colours
media.caption: The whole app retints from a couple of named tokens — high-contrast and vision-assistive modes included. Theming that bends without breaking.

sections.heading: What I'd do differently
sections.body: It's built for a room, not an auditorium. The next real problem is scale: capacity and a grid that stays legible past a dozen faces. I'd design the large-call view as its own mode rather than stretching the small-call one.

weaver.title: Media that learns your taste
weaver.summary: A single-person discovery engine. It learns your taste from what you've already liked, then goes looking for new images you haven't seen, ranked to that taste.
highlights: Turns everything you've saved into taste signal, then finds work you haven't seen.
OpenCLIP embeddings in pgvector, ranked by distance with room kept for exploration.
Shipped as a PWA on a $0 infrastructure budget.

hero.caption: The feed: new work pulled from open sources, ranked to your taste.

sections.heading: The problem
sections.body: Saving things is easy; the apps are already full of your taste. But every feed just shows more of what you've seen, or what's popular — this was my personal problem with my Pinterest feed. I wanted the opposite: use everything I've saved as a signal, and spend it on finding new work I'd never have surfaced myself.

sections.heading: The pivot
sections.body: The first version consolidated your own saves into one feed. It worked, and it was pointless; re-showing pins you already have isn't discovery. So I split every image into two roles: your saves became taste signal, never shown; the feed became new candidates pulled from open sources, ranked against that taste. Same machinery, opposite purpose.

media.alt: The tune-your-taste panel: add an interest, and more/less-like-this concept chips
media.caption: When the feed gets it wrong you don't wait it out — you tell it, in words: more of this, less of that.

sections.heading: How it works
sections.body: Images are embedded with OpenCLIP; your taste is a handful of centroids clustered from your saves; new candidates are scored by how closely they land, with room kept for exploration so the feed never collapses into one look. Save something new and it tightens the profile.

media.alt: An expanded image with like / not-my-taste controls and a 'threads from this' grid of similar work
media.caption: Every image is a thread you can pull. 'Threads from this' is the similarity model itself, made visible.

sections.heading: Constraints & craft
sections.body: Built as a PWA to feel native on a phone, on a $0 infrastructure budget (a recurring theme across my exploratory projects): Supabase and pgvector for storage and search, a daily job to refresh the pool. Motion is layered but every layer is gated on reduced-motion, and accessibility (semantic markup, focus management, alt text, AA contrast) was a starting point, not a finish.

media.alt: The Weaver feed on a phone: a two-column masonry with some cards tilted like pinned scraps
media.caption: Built to live on a phone — a hand-pinned feel, not a stamped-out grid.

sections.heading: What I'd do differently
sections.body: I cut the scheduled aggregation and a learned ranker to ship. Taste refreshes manually and ranks by distance to the centroids, which is honest but blunt. A lightweight learned re-ranker on top of the CLIP similarity is the upgrade I would make next.

booking-room.title: Meeting room booking
booking-room.summary: A meeting-room booking system built to make finding and holding a room take seconds. Search by time, capacity, and amenities; drag to create; and never double-book.
highlights: Find, hold, and never double-book a room in seconds.
Server-side conflict detection, recurring bookings, and ICS export.
Per-room utilisation reports that roll up across a building or the whole org.

hero.caption: The dashboard: your day, your rooms, and quick actions in one place.

sections.heading: The problem
sections.body: Booking a room is usually more clicks than the meeting deserves: hunt a calendar, guess a free slot, hope no one grabbed it first. I wanted the whole thing to take seconds: find a room that fits, hold it, move on.

sections.heading: My role
sections.body: A solo design-and-build — but not a solo guess:
list: Designed and built the whole thing — the calendar interactions, the room finder, and the conflict logic underneath.
Ran UX research and feedback rounds, so the flows matched how people actually book a room, not how I assumed they would.
Owned every trade-off myself, from the data model down to which features to leave out.

sections.heading: What I designed
sections.body: Day, week, and month calendars you can drag on to create a booking. A room finder that filters by date, time, capacity, and amenities. Keyboard shortcuts for the two things people do most: book a room, find a room. And a booking drawer that keeps the details and the conversation in one place.

media.caption: Day, week, and month views, drag to create.

sections.heading: Holding the room, not fighting for it
list: Conflict detection in the database, not just the UI: overlaps are blocked server-side, and a meeting ending at 11:00 doesn't block an 11:00 start.
Recurring bookings, past-slot guards, and ICS export so it lives in your real calendar.
Counter-propose: an approver can offer a different time instead of a flat no.
A comment thread per booking, scoped to the people actually involved.

sections.heading: Built for an organisation
sections.body: Locations, buildings, floors, and rooms in one hierarchy, each with its own timezone. Three roles (admin, booking user, view-only) and an approval queue for rooms that need it. Every change is written to an audit log.

media.alt: Rooms grid with search and filters
media.caption: Rooms by capacity, amenities, and status.

sections.heading: Reports & analytics
sections.body: Bookings are only worth capturing if someone can learn from them. Each room reports its own utilisation — how often it's booked, when demand peaks, and how much of its capacity actually gets used — and the numbers roll up across a building or the whole organisation. Facilities can see which rooms earn their space and which sit empty, and plan from real usage instead of a hunch.

media.caption: Each room carries its own detail and utilisation — what gets used, when, and how hard.

sections.heading: What I'd do differently
sections.body: The four-level location hierarchy is the right model for a big organisation and overkill for a single office. I would add a fast path (one room, one calendar) so a small team never has to meet the admin panel.
